sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Rapid antigen tests detect selected respiratory pathogens at the point of care. In prehospital and remote settings they can help with infection-control planning, cohorting, and onward advice, but they should not override the clinical picture.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
- Follow the specific kit instructions for storage, specimen type, collection method, and read time
- Use appropriate PPE and infection-control precautions throughout collection and disposal
- Confirm patient identity and document the kit type, time, and result
- If the test is invalid, repeat with a new kit if clinically useful and available

sections.title: Result Interpretation
sections.content:
- A positive result in a symptomatic patient usually supports the suspected diagnosis and should trigger appropriate infection-control actions
- A negative result does not reliably exclude infection, especially early in illness or when clinical suspicion is high
- Clinical severity, deterioration, dehydration, hypoxia, or sepsis concern should drive escalation, not the RAT result alone

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- RATs are most useful when they change practical infection-control or cohorting decisions
- Follow local public health, workplace, and reporting requirements where relevant
- Severe illness, hypoxia, dehydration, or sepsis concern should drive transport and escalation even if the RAT is negative
- Record the result, time, and any major limitation such as expired kit, borderline line, or collection difficulty
